body_txt: A subtrope of Sex Signals Death which specifically targets the ladies of the night . Want to stress how depraved and vicious your killer is? Have him — it's almost always 'him' — target and/or kill sex workers. Maybe it's because street-walkers are easy targets. Maybe it's because of the stigma against sex work. Maybe the High-Class Call Girl happened to witness something she shouldn't have (or at least the killer thinks thinks she did). Or maybe the show would rather focus on the Serial Killer and not his victims . I mean, prostitutes go missing all the time... This is Truth in Television to some degree. Prostitution (especially of the street-walking variety) is a vulnerable occupation, as the workers operate outside of legal and social bounds, and must by necessity place their trust in strangers. Simple practicality on the killer's part is often a factor: it's easier to kill someone who must meet you in a secluded place of your choosing with no other witnesses. In all too many cases, women in the sex industry may be a Real Life Serial Killer 's first or only victims. note Indeed, it's not uncommon for the Serial Killer to specifically target prostitutes and actually believe that he's doing society a favor by killing them. The victims are rarely given any kind of characterization outside of their profession. If they are lucky, we'll learn their names, and if the producers really want to hammer home how far they've fallen, we'll probably get some sense of their home life (which will no doubt include drug addiction, abuse, poverty, or even a now-motherless child ). They will usually be a vaguely formed Hooker with a Heart of Gold at best; someone with a conveniently tragic reason to be targeted by a serial killer. Nevertheless, there will be an overtone that these were 'just hookers.' Sometimes we don't even get that: we just get a string of nameless women whose deaths were only meaningful as the run-up to a homicide which is worth investigating. There might even be a sense that they had it coming or were somehow less innocent than other victims because of their occupation; the detectives involved may approach the matter from the standpoint that they can't investigate every dead hooker. This becomes especially glaring when the investigation focuses primarily on the murder of the one woman who was not a prostitute . The sex workers' deaths will be treated as unremarkable or even karmic , but the non-sex worker's death will be treated as tragic. Similarly, if a dead woman is initially mistaken for a sex worker , her death might be taken lightly at first, only for everyone to buckle down and get serious once it comes to light that she was 'actually' an innocent victim. The Disposable Sex Worker has an odd relationship with Missing White Woman Syndrome . On one hand, they're mutually exclusive: the Missing White Woman is "normal" and "wholesome" while a sex worker is neither (especially if she is not White). On the other hand, male and male-to-female transgender sex workers almost never get a mention in fiction or real life. And even though they are just as likely as ciswomen to become victims of violent crime, authorities and the general public are even less likely to care what happens to them. Disposable Vagrant is often the male counterpart. Contrast like hell with Platonic Prostitution and Unproblematic Prostitution .
